segments.en: Good learning happens through making mistakes.
segments.zh: 好的学习是通过犯错误来实现的。

segments.en: Just seeing something many times doesn't mean you'll remember it well.
segments.zh: 仅仅看到某件事多次并不意味着你会记住它。

segments.en: You've seen the Apple logo so many times, of course you know all the features.
segments.zh: 您已经多次看到 Apple 徽标，当然您知道所有功能。

segments.en: But then when you quiz people and test them, and I do this in my class,

segments.en: people aren't sure it's the bite on the left or the right hand side. Is there a stem or a leaf?

segments.en: The best way to remember something is to, again, failures.
segments.zh: 记住某件事的最好方法就是再次失败。

segments.en: I'll have you draw it without looking at it, and you're struggling, and wait, is it on the left or right?
segments.zh: 我让你看都不看就画出来，你就挣扎着，等等，是在左边还是在右边？

segments.en: And so there's a stem or a leaf, and you're starting to question all of these things.
segments.zh: 所以有一根茎或一片叶子，你开始质疑所有这些事情。

segments.en: Then when you look at the logo again, you're going to engage in better learning than if you hadn't

segments.en: done that errorful kind of trial beforehand. Welcome to the Huberman Lab podcast where we discuss

segments.en: up with new ideas and plans for our lives.

segments.en: cognitive abilities well into their 80s and 90s and even beyond, and what any of us can

segments.en: do to improve our chances of maintaining or even getting better at memory as we age.

segments.zh: 事实上，虽然大多数人认为记忆力下降是获得记忆的必然结果。

segments.en: older, Dr. Castell explains research that tells us the story is far more nuanced and
segments.zh: 卡斯特尔博士年长一些，他解释说，研究告诉我们，这个故事要微妙得多，而且

segments.en: memory performance rivals that of people decades younger.

segments.en: You're going to tell us how to build up and keep our memory.

segments.en: But let's start by asking, what is memory, or rather, how do you conceptualize memory?
segments.zh: 但让我们首先问，什么是记忆，或者更确切地说，你如何概念化记忆？

segments.en: From the standpoint of we move through life, we experience things, let's call it passively,
segments.zh: 从我们生活的角度来看，我们被动地经历事物，

segments.en: or we try to remember certain things, what determines what information we hold on to,
segments.zh: 或者我们试图记住某些事情，是什么决定了我们保留哪些信息，

segments.en: how long, and so on.
segments.zh: 多长时间，等等。

segments.en: It's a great question.
segments.zh: 这是一个很好的问题。

segments.en: And memory is really a mental representation of the past.
segments.zh: 记忆实际上是过去的心理表征。

segments.en: And by its very nature, it's reconstructive.
segments.zh: 就其本质而言，它是重建性的。

segments.en: It's never always accurate.
segments.zh: 它并不总是准确的。

segments.en: And I think that's why it's so mysterious and fascinating that you and I can see the same
segments.zh: 我认为这就是为什么它如此神秘和迷人，以至于你和我都能看到同样的东西

segments.en: event and then remember it differently a week or a day later.
segments.zh: 事件，然后一周或一天后以不同的方式记住它。

segments.en: And it starts kind of almost at the sensory level and sometimes our mind plays tricks on
segments.zh: 它几乎从感官层面开始，有时我们的大脑会玩弄花招

segments.en: us.
segments.zh: 我们。

segments.en: And that's how I got interested in this field.
segments.zh: 这就是我对这个领域感兴趣的原因。

segments.en: How can I think I know something and yet not know it as well as I do?
segments.zh: 我怎么可能认为我知道某件事，但实际上却不了解它呢？

segments.en: And this is something that's called metacognition, our awareness of our own cognition.
segments.zh: 这就是所谓的元认知，即我们对自己认知的认识。

segments.en: And as a personal anecdote, when, you know, as in fifth or sixth grade, I had one line
segments.zh: 作为个人轶事，你知道，在五年级或六年级时，我有一句话

segments.en: in the play, The Wizard of Oz.
segments.zh: 在剧《绿野仙踪》中。

segments.en: Hurry and get into the cellar.

segments.en: And, of course, on opening night, listen, everyone, there's a cellar on the way.

segments.en: Hurry and get into the cyclone.

segments.en: Nice.

segments.zh: 我在想，我是如何做到这一点的？

segments.en: Having memorized this line, most of the audience probably didn't even notice.
segments.zh: 记住这句台词后，大多数观众可能都没有注意到。

segments.en: But that to me was an early exposure to memories not always kind of a function of what you
segments.zh: 但对我来说，这是早期接触记忆的过程，并不总是与你所经历的事情有关。

segments.en: do with the information.
segments.zh: 处理信息。

segments.en: And then, in high school, I had another exposure to memory
segments.zh: 然后，在高中时，我再次接触到记忆

segments.en: where I just memorized a lot of things.
segments.zh: 在那里我记住了很多东西。

segments.en: And that got me pretty far in algebra and chemistry.

segments.en: that I really didn't have a strong understanding
segments.zh: 我真的没有很强的理解力

segments.en: of chemistry, even though I could use all these terms.

segments.zh: 我意识到人们研究学习和记忆，

segments.en: and kind of false memories and why memory can
segments.zh: 以及一些错误的记忆以及为什么记忆可以

segments.en: and cannot be accurate.

segments.en: I found that so fascinating.

segments.en: Both impressed and intrigued us more
segments.zh: 既让我们印象深刻，又让我们着迷

segments.en: about memorization of the periodic table.
segments.zh: 关于元素周期表的记忆。

segments.en: First question is about mnemonics and ways
segments.zh: 第一个问题是关于助记符和方式

segments.en: that we try and batch and learn information
segments.zh: 我们尝试批量学习信息

segments.en: by pairing it to other things.
segments.zh: 通过将其与其他事物配对。

segments.en: Trying to remember a room full of people's names
segments.zh: 试图记住一个满是人们名字的房间

segments.en: and you compare the first letter of their name with a fruit,
segments.zh: 然后你将他们名字的第一个字母与水果进行比较，

segments.en: or what, you know, people do these things.

segments.en: To me, that seems like a very inefficient way to learn

segments.en: because you're adding more information
segments.zh: 因为您要添加更多信息

segments.en: that you need to store away.

segments.en: So the first question is,
segments.zh: 所以第一个问题是，

segments.en: is that the best way to learn large batches of information?

segments.en: I think it's almost a workaround,
segments.zh: 我认为这几乎是一个解决方法

segments.zh: 这就是人们说的第一类问题

segments.en: is I can't remember names.
segments.zh: 是我不记得名字了。

segments.en: And by their nature, names used to be descriptive
segments.zh: 就其本质而言，名称过去具有描述性

segments.en: of person's occupation.

segments.en: someone's a baker, we call them Mr. Baker.
segments.zh: 有人是面包师，我们称他们为贝克先生。

segments.en: But that's, of course, evolved with time.
segments.zh: 但这当然是随着时间的推移而演变的。

segments.en: And names, I tell people, if you can't remember someone's name,
segments.zh: 还有名字，我告诉人们，如果你记不住某人的名字，

segments.en: that's probably a natural forgetting,
segments.zh: 这可能是一种自然的遗忘

segments.en: but you remember if you had an interesting conversation,
segments.zh: 但你记得如果你们有过一次有趣的谈话，

segments.en: or you remember if you can trust them,
segments.zh: 或者你记得你是否可以信任他们，

segments.en: that's probably more important.
segments.zh: 这可能更重要。

segments.en: So then to engage our kind of knowledge base
segments.zh: 那么就利用我们的知识库

segments.en: so that we can remember names,
segments.zh: 以便我们能够记住名字，

segments.en: we need to make this arbitrary link.
segments.zh: 我们需要建立这个任意链接。

segments.en: And so I tell people my last name is pastel, castel.
segments.zh: 所以我告诉人们我的姓氏是pastel、castel。

segments.en: Castel rhymes with pastel on my grandfather used pastels, let's say.
segments.zh: 可以说，卡斯特尔与粉彩押韵，我祖父用粉彩。

segments.en: And so people can then make this link.
segments.zh: 这样人们就可以建立这个链接。

segments.en: But like you say, that's effortful.
segments.zh: 但就像你说的，这很费力。

segments.en: It takes time.

segments.en: It takes time away from kind of the essence of something else we could talk about.

segments.en: And it also leads to these kind of predictable memory errors.
segments.zh: 它还会导致这些可预测的记忆错误。

segments.en: So people say, oh, I remember his name was pastel.
segments.zh: 所以人们说，哦，我记得他的名字是柔和的。

segments.en: Or in fact, it's castel.
segments.zh: 或者事实上，它是卡斯特。

segments.en: So I think we have to almost trick our memory into remembering things that might be somewhat
segments.zh: 所以我认为我们几乎必须欺骗我们的记忆来记住一些可能有点

segments.en: arbitrary.
segments.zh: 任意的。

segments.en: And that becomes more pronounced as we get older.
segments.zh: 随着年龄的增长，这种情况变得更加明显。

segments.en: There's more Andrews and it's just more interference.
segments.zh: 安德鲁斯更多了，干扰也更多了。

segments.en: And so to get around that, we have to use deliberate practice,
segments.zh: 因此，为了解决这个问题，我们必须进行刻意练习，

segments.en: retrieval practice, use the name several times.
segments.zh: 检索练习中，多次使用该名称。

segments.en: But I tell my students whose names I often forget
segments.zh: 但我告诉我的学生，他们的名字我经常忘记

segments.en: that I'll still remember topics we've talked about
segments.zh: 我仍然会记得我们谈论过的话题

segments.en: or how they felt about something.
segments.zh: 或者他们对某事的感受。

segments.en: And I think that's more important,
segments.zh: 而且我认为更重要的是

segments.en: sometimes just remembering a name.
segments.zh: 有时只是记住一个名字。

segments.en: To remember chemistry or some other subject
segments.zh: 记住化学或其他一些科目

segments.en: involves nomenclature, not everyday terms, unless you're a chemist, but to really understand
segments.zh: 涉及命名法，不是日常术语，除非您是化学家，但要真正理解

segments.en: the deeper mechanics of that thing and remember that, what are some better tools?
segments.zh: 更深入的机制并记住，有哪些更好的工具？

segments.en: I want to say, and I'm not a memory researcher, that it might be helpful to think in terms
segments.zh: 我想说，我不是记忆研究者，用术语来思考可能会有所帮助

segments.en: of verbs, not labels.
segments.zh: 动词，而不是标签。

segments.en: So rather than thinking about a particular element, just in terms of its name, but think
segments.zh: 因此，不要仅仅考虑某个特定元素的名称，而是考虑

segments.en: about how it interacts with something else or how it gives or receives a charge in certain
segments.zh: 关于它如何与其他事物相互作用或它如何在某些情况下给予或接收电荷

segments.en: conditions.
segments.zh: 条件。

segments.en: Absolutely. I think that's what you're looking for is deeper learning, deeper semantic learning.
segments.zh: 绝对地。我认为这就是你正在寻找的更深层次的学习，更深层次的语义学习。

segments.en: This has been referred to as levels of processing. I was working at a very low level of processing
segments.zh: 这被称为处理级别。我的处理水平非常低

segments.en: when I'm making up happy Henry for hydrogen and helium. But if you can start to visualize
segments.zh: 当我给快乐亨利补氢和氦时。但如果你能开始想象

segments.en: things or interact with them, and that's why we want to have chemistry labs or places where
segments.zh: 事物或与它们互动，这就是为什么我们想要有化学实验室或地方

segments.en: I found chemistry very abstract.
segments.zh: 我发现化学非常抽象。

segments.en: I think that's why I didn't really engage with it,
segments.zh: 我想这就是我没有真正参与其中的原因

segments.en: but I found psychology to be something
segments.zh: 但我发现心理学是一种东西

segments.en: that we were interacting with every day.
segments.zh: 我们每天都在与之互动。

segments.en: I was making these memory errors.
segments.zh: 我犯了这些记忆错误。

segments.en: I saw my parents and grandparents remembering
segments.zh: 我看到我的父母和祖父母在回忆

segments.en: and forgetting things, and I thought,
segments.zh: 忘记事情，我想，

segments.zh: 这是怎么发生的？

segments.zh: 大脑是如此复杂，我不认为

segments.en: will ever completely understand
segments.zh: 永远会完全明白

segments.en: why this memory error might happen.
segments.zh: 为什么会发生这个内存错误。

segments.en: But I think this awareness is really important.

segments.en: on this.
segments.zh: 关于这一点。

segments.en: You learn a lot by making these mistakes.

segments.en: I think that illustrates also how our memory works, that just seeing something many times
segments.zh: 我认为这也说明了我们的记忆是如何运作的，即多次看到某件事

segments.zh: 并不意味着你会记得很好。

segments.zh: 这是一个你已经见过很多次的经典演示。

segments.zh: 我已将其更新为 Apple 徽标。

segments.en: You've seen the Apple logo so many times.
segments.zh: 您已经多次看到 Apple 徽标。

segments.en: Of course, you know all the features.
